title: Pivot or Persevere Decision
summary: A structured decision framework for when things are not working and you need to decide: change direction or double down.
whenToUseIt: When growth has stalled, a major assumption has been invalidated, or you are questioning whether the current path is right.

steps.title: State the situation honestly
steps.description: Write down: What is not working? How long has it not been working? What have you already tried to fix it? What data supports the conclusion that it is not working?
steps.tips: Distinguish between 'not working yet' and 'fundamentally broken'. Traction that is slow but accelerating is different from traction that is flat or declining.

steps.title: Test your core assumptions
steps.description: List the 3-5 core assumptions your business depends on. For each, rate whether it has been validated (strong evidence), partially validated (some evidence), or invalidated (evidence against). Examples: 'Customers will pay $X for this', 'This problem is painful enough to switch', 'We can acquire customers at $Y CAC'.

steps.title: Assess what a pivot would look like
steps.description: If you were to change direction, what would you change? Options: pivot the customer (same product, different market), pivot the problem (same market, different pain), pivot the solution (same problem, different approach), pivot the channel (same everything, different GTM). Write out 2-3 concrete pivot options.

steps.title: Run the regret test
steps.description: Imagine it is 12 months from now. Scenario A: you persevered and it worked. Scenario B: you persevered and it did not work. Scenario C: you pivoted and it worked. Scenario D: you pivoted and it did not work. Which scenario do you regret most? This reveals your risk tolerance.

steps.title: Make the call and commit
steps.description: Based on the evidence and your analysis, choose: persevere with specific changes, or pivot to a specific new direction. Write down what you are committing to, what success looks like in 90 days, and what signal would trigger a re-evaluation.
steps.tips: The worst outcome is not a wrong decision — it is no decision. Half-pivoting kills companies faster than a clear wrong choice.

expectedOutcome: A clear, evidence-based decision to either persevere with specific changes or pivot to a defined new direction, with a 90-day success criteria and re-evaluation trigger.
commonMistakes: Making the decision based on emotion rather than evidence
Half-pivoting — changing direction without fully committing
Confusing a distribution problem with a product problem
Not setting a re-evaluation date — you need a checkpoint
